blocks.text: Chapter 7.
Superteams: When Groups Beat Their Best Member
The ideas in 30 seconds
Groups can make people dumber (groupthink) or smarter (wisdom of crowds); the outcome depends entirely on process, not on the raw talent in the room.
In the Good Judgment Project, forecasters randomly assigned to teams were 23% more accurate than individuals working alone, and teams of superforecasters beat prediction markets by 15-30%.
The antidote to groupthink is engineered dissent: precision questioning, constructive confrontation, devil's advocates, and leaders who withhold their own views.
Adam Grant's giver/taker research explains superteam culture: generous sharing of information raises everyone's accuracy without letting free riders sink the group.
Scott Page's "diversity trumps ability" logic shows why a cognitively varied team can outperform a team of clones of its single best member — different errors cancel out.

The tournament's single best forecasting method was an algorithm that aggregated many judgments and then extremized them, pushing the average away from 50% to recover the confidence lost when knowledge is dispersed.
Groupthink: How the Best and Brightest Blessed the Bay of Pigs

In April 1961, John F. Kennedy's famously brilliant team approved a CIA plan to land roughly 1,400 Cuban exiles at the Bay of Pigs and topple Fidel Castro. The plan collapsed within days: the invaders were pinned on the beach and captured. Its assumptions were absurd on inspection — for example, the fallback plan that a failed invasion force could melt into the mountains ignored eighty miles of impassable swamp between the landing site and those mountains. Nobody at the table checked a map with the plan's premises in mind.

Psychologist Irving Janis studied the fiasco and coined groupthink: in cohesive groups, members unconsciously protect consensus because harmony feels good and dissent feels like betrayal. People self-censor doubts, assume silence means agreement, and converge on the majority (or the leader's) view without ever testing it. Critically, groupthink is not stupidity — it happened to some of the most credentialed advisers ever assembled. It is a predictable byproduct of small-group psychology when nothing in the process forces disagreement into the open.

This is the standard indictment of committees, and it explains why many people assume forecasting is best done alone: a group might anchor everyone on the first confident voice, add social pressure to conform, and turn independent estimates into an echo. But there is an equally strong case for groups. Chapter 3 covered Galton's wisdom-of-crowds result: aggregating independent judgments pools dispersed scraps of information that no single person holds. A well-run group can do even better than passive aggregation, because members can actively share information, challenge each other's assumptions, and catch each other's blind spots.
So which effect wins — the echo chamber or the information pool? Tetlock's team treated this as an empirical question and, in the spirit of the whole book, ran the experiment.
The Same Men, a Smarter Process: JFK's Redesign

The most striking evidence that process is what matters comes from Kennedy himself. Eighteen months after the Bay of Pigs, largely the same group of advisers handled the Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 — and performed brilliantly, steering between hawks demanding air strikes and the risk of nuclear war, ultimately settling on a blockade and a negotiated withdrawal of Soviet missiles.

The difference was a deliberately redesigned decision process. After the Bay of Pigs, Kennedy ordered an inquiry into his own decision-making and rebuilt it to institutionalize skepticism. The new rules: participants were told to act as skeptical generalists, questioning everything rather than defending departmental turf; formal agenda and rank were suspended to loosen hierarchy; the group sometimes split into subgroups that developed rival options and then cross-examined each other; and Kennedy periodically left the room, because he knew his presence made people bend toward what they thought he wanted to hear.

The lesson is precise: the same brains that produced groupthink produced excellent collective judgment once the process made dissent mandatory instead of costly. Groups don't have fixed IQs; they have designs, and designs can be fixed.

Industry discovered the same principle. At Intel, Andy Grove enforced a culture of constructive confrontation — vigorous, unsparing debate aimed at ideas rather than people, in which challenging the boss's view was an obligation, not a career risk. The pairing of words matters: confrontation without the constructive norm degenerates into ego wars, while constructiveness without confrontation drifts back into polite groupthink. A functional team must hold both.
The Experiment: Teams Beat Individuals by 23%

The Good Judgment Project turned the group question into a randomized trial. Starting in year two, some forecasters were randomly assigned to work alone, others to collaborate in online teams that could share information, argue, and see each other's forecasts. If groupthink dominated, teams should have done worse. They didn't: on average, teams were 23% more accurate than individuals.

The GJP stacked the deck against groupthink with light-touch process design. Teams received a primer on group pitfalls and were explicitly encouraged to challenge one another — to treat disagreement as a resource rather than a rupture. One recommended tool was precision questioning, a technique taught by Dennis Matthies: instead of attacking a vague claim head-on, drill into its components — "What exactly do you mean by 'unlikely'? What evidence supports that piece? How does that follow?" Precision questioning surfaces hidden assumptions and lets colleagues critique a forecast without it feeling like a personal assault.

Then came the stronger test. When superforecasters were grouped into teams of roughly a dozen, these superteams dominated everything else in the tournament. They beat ordinary teams decisively; they were around 15-30% more accurate than prediction markets, in which forecasters traded contracts on outcomes and prices aggregated their beliefs — the mechanism many economists expected to be unbeatable. And superforecasters kept improving inside teams rather than regressing, suggesting the collaboration itself sharpened their thinking.

Team life was not frictionless. Two failure modes constantly threatened: the group could become too polite, rubber-stamping each other's numbers to preserve warmth, or too abrasive, driving members away. The best teams navigated between them — sustaining the perpetual-beta ethic from Chapter 6 collectively, running postmortems on shared failures instead of assigning blame.
Givers, Takers, and the Culture of Sharing

Why did superteams work when so many committees fail? Wharton psychologist Adam Grant's research on givers, takers, and matchers supplies part of the answer. Givers contribute more than they extract; takers do the reverse; matchers calibrate tit for tat. Grant found givers at both extremes of performance distributions — exploited givers sink, but strategic givers rise to the top, because generosity compounds across a network.

Superteams were built on giver behavior. Members like Doug Lorch (the pattern held across top teams) invested hours hunting down research and sharing it with teammates who might never reciprocate. In a purely competitive frame that looks irrational — you're arming your rivals. Inside a team scored on collective accuracy, it is exactly rational: one member's legwork raises every forecast, and cultures of sharing proved contagious. Norms of generosity, not just aggregation math, drove the 23% edge.

Culture also had to protect independent thought. The danger of sharing is correlation — if everyone reads the same teammate's analysis, the team's forecasts stop being independent draws and the crowd's error-cancelling magic weakens. Effective superteams countered this by prizing active open-mindedness at the group level: treating one another's views as hypotheses to test, keeping dissenters engaged, and explicitly inviting the question "what am I missing?" A team, like an individual, can have the fox-like temperament described in Chapter 2 — or fail to.
Diversity Trumps Ability — and the Algorithm That Won the Tournament

Economist Scott Page formalized why teams shouldn't be built from clones of the best member. His diversity-trumps-ability result: under broad conditions, a collection of diverse problem-solvers outperforms a collection of the highest-ability problem-solvers, because clones share the same blind spots and make correlated errors, while diverse thinkers err in different directions and cancel each other out. Ability still matters — you want skilled and diverse — but adding a different perspective can improve a group more than adding another copy of its star. Superteams embodied this: retired programmers, pharmacists, film directors, and social scientists attacking the same question from different angles.

The tournament's final twist pushed aggregation one step further. GJP's winning method pooled thousands of forecasts — weighting better and more recent forecasters more heavily — and then extremized the result: pushed the aggregate away from 50% toward 0 or 100 (a pooled 70% might become 85%). The logic: each forecaster holds only a fragment of the total available information. If every fragment could be given to one person, that person would be more confident than any individual actually is; averaging alone bakes in the timidity caused by dispersed knowledge. Extremizing simulates the confidence of a fully informed forecaster.

It worked spectacularly. The extremized aggregate of GJP forecasts beat every rival method in the IARPA tournament, including prediction markets and, per the widely reported result, intelligence analysts with access to classified information. One caveat: extremizing pays off only when knowledge is genuinely dispersed. If everyone already shares the same information — as in a tight superteam that has pooled everything — their consensus is already extremized, and pushing further adds error. The formula, like every tool in the book, works only when you understand why it works.

The chapter's arc completes the book's aggregation theme: an individual can synthesize perspectives dragonfly-style (Chapter 3); a well-designed team synthesizes across people; an algorithm synthesizes across teams. At every level, the winning move is the same — combine diverse, independent judgments and stop any single voice, including your own, from drowning out the rest.
In practice
Before your next group decision, collect written, independent estimates from every participant first, then discuss — aggregation only works if judgments start uncorrelated.
If you lead, state your view last or leave the room, JFK-style; assign someone to argue the opposing case so dissent is a role, not a risk.
Replace "I disagree" with precision questioning: ask a colleague to define their key term, name the evidence behind one component, and walk through the inferential step you doubt.
Adopt Grove's constructive-confrontation norm explicitly: tell your team that challenging ideas — including the boss's — is expected, and that attacks on people are out of bounds.
Recruit for cognitive diversity, not clones of your best performer: add the person whose background and error patterns differ from the group's, then weight their input seriously.
When you pool several independent probability estimates, try extremizing the average modestly toward 0 or 100 — but only if the estimators drew on genuinely different information.
